Her symptoms are a textbook manifestation of a gastric outlet obstruction caused by a mobile intragastric mass, leading to a ball-valve effect.

Once the patient vomits, the stomach decompresses (and the mass may fall back into the gastric body), providing temporary relief from the pain.

The severe paroxysms of pain—lasting 2 to 15 minutes, occurring abruptly—are mechanical colicky pain, resulting from forceful gastric peristalsis attempting to push an indigestible mass through the pylorus.
A critical imaging clue is the CT finding.
A patient who has a decreased appetite, has been in severe distress, and has vomited at least five times on the day of presentation should have an empty, decompressed stomach.

problem_representation: A 32-year-old previously healthy woman presents with subacute progressive constitutional symptoms (fatigue, myalgias) and cardiac conduction abnormalities (first-degree and Mobitz type I AV block) developing 5 weeks after SARS-CoV-2 reinfection, with associated transient transaminitis, elevated D-dimer, pleuritic/positional chest pain, and notable exposure history (forested area, livestock contact, outdoor activities).

Evidence of intestinal inflammation/bleeding: positive fecal occult blood.

The patient is a 17-year-old girl presenting with a classic, severe pulmonary-renal syndrome.

In an adolescent, the differential for severe pulmonary-renal syndrome is mainly anchored around ANCA-associated vasculitides (AAV), Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE), and Anti-GBM (Goodpasture's) disease.

Her clinical progression is telling: she began with a prodrome of rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, sore throat, and bilateral eye redness that evolved into a lingering unilateral right eye "soreness." This pattern points straight away to upper airway and ophthalmologic involvement—distinct hallmarks of GPA.
This pattern points straight away to upper airway and ophthalmologic involvement—distinct hallmarks of GPA.
The "sore eye" is particularly evocative of episcleritis or scleritis, very common in GPA but relatively absent in Anti-GBM disease.

the core facts remain steadfast: this patient unmistakably has a devastating combination of diffuse alveolar hemorrhage and actively nephritic, rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis

the unique symptom cascade (progressing from specific upper-respiratory and ocular symptoms like developing unilaterally somatic "soreness" in an eye, followed by massive pulmonary-renal failure) elegantly frames the pathophysiological trajectory of GPA.
her initial syndrome could have been no more than a simple viral URI that tipped over the immune dominoes to trigger Anti-GBM disease
the prominent features of AAV mimic true infections so well that patients repeatedly receive empiric antibiotics before the full immune syndrome defines itself
as seen with this patient receiving multiple rounds of outpatient medications/antibiotics
Therefore, the initial clinical triad still positions GPA as the prevailing most-likely diagnosis.
Post-infectious glomerulonephritis (with secondary diffuse alveolar hemorrhage or severe heart failure)

reflection_events.trigger_description: The model reconsidered GPA because the patient was an adolescent with an exceedingly swift 10-day viral-like coryza prodrome, raising concern that the initial URI symptoms were unrelated and that Anti-GBM disease might better explain the explosive pulmonary-renal syndrome.

nodes.label: 46-year-old man who has sex with men (MSM)

edges.verbatim: Epidemiology: MSM with recent new sexual partner
5 months prior - perfect timing for secondary stage
Rash: Papulosquamous, involving trunk and extremities
"Moth-eaten" alopecia: Highly specific for syphilis

Nasal symptoms: Can indicate early gummatous involvement or mucous membrane involvement
Arthritis: Syphilitic polyarthritis is well-documented in secondary stage

edges.verbatim: Recurrent, severe, abrupt abdominal pain over weeks with repeatedly normal imaging and no obstruction, appendicitis, biliary disease, or gynecologic pathology.

Normal liver chemistries, renal function, urinalysis, and CT/ultrasound despite severe symptoms support a functional/metabolic cause.

nodes.label: Accidental (environmental) hypothermia due to ethanol intoxication, complicated by severe frostbite
Alcohol intoxication as the primary cause of altered consciousness (with secondary exposure)
Traumatic brain injury/concussion or intracranial hemorrhage (fall/assault) contributing to collapse and exposure
Hypoglycemia contributing to altered mental status
Acute pancreatitis (alcohol- and/or hypothermia-associated) as a concurrent process
cold, anesthetic, bullous extremities with absent distal flow
Ethanol level is in the toxic range
impaired judgment/immobility and loss of shivering
moderate hypothermia (31.3°C) with bradycardia and Osborn wave
hemorrhagic bullae, anesthesia, dusky/mottled tissue, absent distal perfusion
Severe frostbite signs: hemorrhagic bullae + anesthesia + absent Doppler flow distally
prolonged immobilization, cold injury, and repeated falls
ethanol level is very high and could by itself explain unconsciousness/immobility
abrasions raise concern for trauma/assault as a primary event
severe frostbite still require significant exposure
persistent altered mental status, worsening headache, vomiting, focal deficits, or failure to improve with rewarming
Frostbite severity markers (hemorrhagic bullae, anesthesia, absent distal flow)
Lipase/amylase elevation and leukocytosis are common “noise” in hypothermia/critical stress
Accidental environmental hypothermia with severe frostbite due to ethanol intoxication, complicated by rhabdomyolysis (and possible evolving tissue necrosis/vascular thrombosis in digits/foot)
Ethanol intoxication (primary precipitant of impaired thermoregulation/judgment/immobility)

defining discriminators (exposure + hypothermia ECG + paradoxical undressing + severe frostbite physiology)

Ethanol level is in the toxic range; intoxication plausibly led to impaired judgment/immobility and loss of shivering → exposure injury.
Forehead abrasions and repeated falls; must consider occult head injury in an unconscious patient found outdoors.

reflection_events.trigger_description: Very high ethanol level, abrasions suggesting trauma/assault, and elevated lipase/amylase, leukocytosis, and mild hypoglycemia not fully explained by hypothermia/frostbite alone triggered reconsideration of whether intoxication or trauma was primary.

problem_representation: Previously healthy 16-year-old found outdoors after prolonged cold exposure (−15°C with wind chill −29°C) following binge alcohol use, presenting with **moderate accidental hypothermia** (T 31.3°C, bradycardia, Osborn wave, no shivering) and **severe peripheral cold injury** (dusky/mottled anesthetic hands/feet with hemorrhagic bullae and absent distal perfusion), plus **rhabdomyolysis** and mild metabolic derangements.
final_diagnosis: Accidental environmental hypothermia with severe frostbite precipitated by ethanol intoxication

edges.verbatim: The pattern is hepatocellular, not cholestatic: AST/ALT near and then above 1000 with normal alkaline phosphatase.
She has jaundice and pruritus, but the biochemical pattern still points to primary hepatocellular injury with cholestatic symptoms from conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
The postpartum state is a classic setting for the emergence or flare of autoimmune disease, including autoimmune hepatitis.
Serologies are not strongly positive, but anti-smooth-muscle antibody 1:80 is meaningful
and ANA is weakly positive. Autoimmune hepatitis can present acutely and may initially have only modest autoantibody titers.
Viral workup is partly negative: HAV IgM, HCV Ab, HIV, EBV, CMV are negative.
HBV remains incompletely excluded, but HBsAg is negative.
Gallstones are present, but no ductal dilation and normal alkaline phosphatase make obstructive disease less likely as the primary process.
Gallstones are present, but no ductal dilation and normal alkaline phosphatase make obstructive disease less likely as the primary process.
Acetaminophen toxicity is possible in principle, but the history suggests only a few doses shortly before recognition of jaundice, and the illness began earlier.
She had a viral prodrome-like illness 2.5 weeks earlier, which could fit either acute viral hepatitis or an immune-triggering event.
She had a viral prodrome-like illness 2.5 weeks earlier, which could fit either acute viral hepatitis or an immune-triggering event.
Total protein was slightly high with normal albumin, potentially suggesting hypergammaglobulinemia, which supports autoimmune hepatitis.
Ceruloplasmin was normal, which lowers but does not eliminate Wilson disease.
The autoantibodies are low titer/borderline, not strongly convincing.
The aminotransferases >1000 also fit acute viral hepatitis, especially hepatitis B, at least as well.
The URI-like prodrome may fit viral hepatitis better.
Pruritus and dark urine are prominent and can make one think of obstructive or viral hepatitis, though they are not incompatible with autoimmune hepatitis.
Pruritus and dark urine are prominent and can make one think of obstructive or viral hepatitis, though they are not incompatible with autoimmune hepatitis.
Pruritus and dark urine are prominent and can make one think of obstructive or viral hepatitis, though they are not incompatible with autoimmune hepatitis.

AST/ALT >1000 with jaundice in a young adult is classic.
A window-period/early acute infection could yield negative HBsAg initially.
The equivocal anti-HBs does not establish immunity and does not exclude acute infection.
Risk factors are limited but not absent: recent pregnancy/sexual exposure history is not fully detailed, and remote body piercing exists.
No anti-HBc IgM result is provided; absence of this key marker limits confirmation.
Autoimmune markers and postpartum setting tilt away from HBV.
Any teenager/young adult with acute hepatitis requires consideration.
Against it: normal ceruloplasmin, normal CBC, no hemolysis mentioned, normal neuro exam, preserved renal function.
Against it: normal ceruloplasmin, normal CBC, no hemolysis mentioned, normal neuro exam, preserved renal function.

Findings most strongly supporting autoimmune hepatitis - Positive ASMA (1:80)
Findings most strongly supporting autoimmune hepatitis - Slight suggestion of hypergammaglobulinemia (high total protein with normal albumin)
Findings most strongly supporting autoimmune hepatitis - Negative broad initial infectious workup
Findings most strongly supporting autoimmune hepatitis - Hepatocellular pattern without convincing obstruction

but the available serologies do not support it yet.
Autoimmune hepatitis has a particularly good epidemiologic fit in a young postpartum woman
and can present acutely with negative/low-titer serologies early.

The autoantibodies are low titer/borderline, not strongly convincing.
The URI-like prodrome may fit viral hepatitis better.
Pruritus and dark urine are prominent and can make one think of obstructive or viral hepatitis
My reassessment does not change the leading diagnosis
though it narrows the margin between autoimmune hepatitis and acute hepatitis B.
Wilson disease remains a less likely but important exclusion in a 19-year-old.
The strongest overall synthesis remains acute autoimmune hepatitis, likely postpartum-triggered, because of the demographic setting, timing after delivery
because of the demographic setting, timing after delivery, hepatocellular injury pattern
because of the demographic setting, timing after delivery, hepatocellular injury pattern, absence of strong evidence for other viral causes
and the presence of anti-smooth-muscle antibody.
Acute hepatitis B remains the key competing diagnosis and would need definitive exclusion with anti-HBc IgM and HBV DNA.

reflection_events.trigger_description: Low-titer autoimmune serologies and a URI-like prodrome with prominent pruritus/dark urine prompted reconsideration of autoimmune hepatitis versus acute viral/obstructive causes, especially hepatitis B.

problem_representation: 19-year-old postpartum woman with a subacute hepatocellular liver injury syndrome characterized by marked jaundice/pruritus and very high aminotransferases (>1000 U/L), conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, minimal alkaline phosphatase elevation, preserved synthetic function except mild PT prolongation, negative initial viral serologies, gallstones without ductal dilation, and new low-titer autoimmune markers.
